content.thinking: Let me look at the chart image first to understand what we're working with.

The chart shows marriage rates per 1,000 people for multiple countries. I can see:
- US (blue/purple at top)
- China (dark red, which peaks around 2013 and then drops steeply)

China's dark red line is particularly striking—it peaks around 2013 and then plummets sharply afterward. Let me zoom in on that section to see the exact trajectory and magnitude of the decline.

That crop didn't work as intended. Let me try to read the chart image more carefully from the initial view. 

Looking at the chart, I can see:
- China (dark red line) peaked around 2013 at what looks like around 9-10 marriages per 1,000 people and then dropped steeply to around 5.9 by 2019
- The other lines (US, Japan, Korea, Australia, UK, Spain, France) all seem to be declining but more gradually

The China line has by far the steepest drop from around 2013 onwards. The line peaks dramatically around 2013 and then falls sharply.


Now I'm reading the legend values on the right side of the chart. China's current rate is 5.9, which confirms that dramatic drop from its 2013 peak of around 9-10. The US started much higher at 8.3 in 2000 and has declined more gradually to 5.7, so while both countries have dropped by similar amounts, China's decline was much more sudden and concentrated in a shorter timeframe.

content.text:
Looking at the chart, I can see multiple countries' marriage rates. The dark red line representing China peaks dramatically around 2013 at approximately 9+ marriages per 1,000 people and then drops sharply to 5.9 by 2019.

<think>
From the chart:
- China's line (dark red) peaks around 2013 at ~9-10 and then falls steeply to ~5.9 by 2019 — a drop of ~3-4 points
- Other countries like US, Japan, Korea, Australia, UK, Spain, France show more gradual declines
- China's drop from 2013 onwards is clearly the steepest of all the countries shown
</think>

<answer>
China has experienced the steepest drop in marriage rate since 2013. Its rate peaked around 2013 at approximately 9–10 marriages per 1,000 people and fell sharply to about 5.9 by 2019, a decline of roughly 3–4 points — far steeper than any other country shown in the chart.
</answer>
